title: REIMAGINING THE WEB: A TECHNICAL AND PHILOSOPHICAL ANALYSIS ON GEMINI CAPSULES AS AN HTTP ALTERNATIV
author: Mr. Shubham Chavan  and Dhanraj C Jadhav
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: The modern World Wide Web (www) is an evolved and complex ecosystem of bloated software, protocol, surveillance, and tracking systems by capitalistic Tech Giants, leading to an enormous energy consumption as well as erosion of individual privacy. This paper explores the Gemini protocol and its extreme lightweightness compared to HTTP, offering a technical and philosophical reimagination of our day-to-day online interactions. Gemini started in the year 2019 with a philosophy which is minimal in nature. This approach prioritizes simplicity, privacy, and sustainability from the start. It uses TLS encryption for every connection by default, and the entire protocol is designed to be minimal. It's all about straightforward requests and responses, unlike HTTP, which has become increasingly complex with countless extensions and features. This is a part of a broader movement called the "Small Web Movement" which pushes back against the Tech Giants who dominates the internet we have ended up with. The idea is to build something more decentralized, designed around actual users rather than advertisers and collecting data. Through technical analysis, this research shows that Gemini is significantly more resource-efficient. Client applications use about 60% less memory than traditional HTTP browsers. Gemini's text-first content format (Gemtext) avoids the processing overhead of JavaScript, CSS, and third-party trackers, reducing energy use and improving user privacy. Our study analyzes protocol specifications, client-server architectures, and realworld performance under various network conditions. The paper concludes by indicating a better protocol for future internet and suggesting a hybrid models that balance innovation with ethical design. Keywords: Gemini protocol, Digital privacy, Internet decentralization, Sustainable architectures, Small Web Movement
